meta.source: NASA/JPL Horizons, NASA Planetary Fact Sheets

bodies.name: Sun
bodies.description: The star at the center of the solar system. A G-type main-sequence star containing 99.86% of the system's mass.

bodies.name: Mercury
bodies.description: The smallest planet and closest to the Sun. Has extreme temperature swings from -180 to 430 C due to virtually no atmosphere.

bodies.name: Venus
bodies.description: Second planet from the Sun. Hottest planet due to extreme greenhouse effect. Rotates retrograde (clockwise when viewed from north).

bodies.name: Earth
bodies.description: Third planet from the Sun. Only known planet with liquid surface water and life.

bodies.name: Moon
bodies.description: Earth's only natural satellite. Tidally locked, showing the same face to Earth. Fifth largest moon in the solar system.

bodies.name: Mars
bodies.description: Fourth planet from the Sun. Known as the Red Planet due to iron oxide on its surface. Home to Olympus Mons, the tallest volcano in the solar system.

bodies.name: Phobos
bodies.description: Larger and innermost moon of Mars. Irregularly shaped, heavily cratered. Orbiting closer to Mars over time and expected to be destroyed in 50 million years.

bodies.name: Deimos
bodies.description: Smaller and outermost moon of Mars. Smoother than Phobos due to regolith filling craters.

bodies.name: Jupiter
bodies.description: Largest planet in the solar system. A gas giant with a mass more than twice that of all other planets combined. Famous for the Great Red Spot storm.

bodies.name: Io
bodies.description: Innermost Galilean moon of Jupiter. Most volcanically active body in the solar system, with over 400 active volcanoes driven by tidal heating.

bodies.name: Europa
bodies.description: Second Galilean moon. Has a smooth ice shell covering a global subsurface ocean, making it a prime candidate for extraterrestrial life.

bodies.name: Ganymede
bodies.description: Largest moon in the solar system, bigger than Mercury. Only moon known to have its own magnetic field. Likely has a subsurface ocean.

bodies.name: Callisto
bodies.description: Outermost Galilean moon. Most heavily cratered object in the solar system. May have a subsurface ocean.

bodies.name: Amalthea
bodies.description: Fifth largest moon of Jupiter. Irregularly shaped and reddish, the reddest object in the solar system.

bodies.name: Himalia
bodies.description: Largest irregular moon of Jupiter. Leads the Himalia group of prograde irregular satellites.

bodies.name: Thebe
bodies.description: Inner moon of Jupiter. Irregularly shaped, tidally locked, and a source of material for Jupiter's gossamer ring.

bodies.name: Elara
bodies.description: Prograde irregular moon of Jupiter, part of the Himalia group.

bodies.name: Metis
bodies.description: Innermost known moon of Jupiter. Orbits within Jupiter's main ring.

bodies.name: Adrastea
bodies.description: Second innermost moon of Jupiter. One of the smallest moons in the solar system.

bodies.name: Saturn

ring_system.composition: water ice with trace rocky material

bodies.description: Sixth planet from the Sun. Famous for its extensive ring system. Least dense planet, would float in water if a body of water large enough existed.

bodies.name: Titan
bodies.description: Largest moon of Saturn, second largest in the solar system. Only moon with a dense atmosphere. Has liquid methane/ethane lakes on its surface.

bodies.name: Enceladus
bodies.description: Sixth-largest moon of Saturn. Has active water geysers at its south pole erupting from a global subsurface ocean. Highest albedo of any body in the solar system.

bodies.name: Mimas
bodies.description: Smallest astronomical body known to be in hydrostatic equilibrium. Famous for the Herschel crater giving it a resemblance to the Death Star.

bodies.name: Tethys
bodies.description: Fifth-largest moon of Saturn. Composed almost entirely of water ice. Features a massive canyon system (Ithaca Chasma) spanning 2,000 km.

bodies.name: Dione
bodies.description: Fourth-largest moon of Saturn. Has a tenuous oxygen atmosphere and may have a subsurface liquid water ocean.

bodies.name: Rhea
bodies.description: Second-largest moon of Saturn. Heavily cratered and composed primarily of water ice. Has a very thin atmosphere of oxygen and carbon dioxide.

bodies.name: Hyperion

discovery.discoverer: William Cranch Bond, George Phillips Bond, William Lassell

bodies.description: Irregularly shaped moon of Saturn with a sponge-like appearance. Has chaotic rotation with no fixed rotational period.

bodies.name: Iapetus
bodies.description: Third-largest moon of Saturn. Famous for its two-toned coloring: one hemisphere is dark as coal, the other bright as snow. Has a prominent equatorial ridge.

bodies.name: Phoebe
bodies.description: Retrograde irregular moon of Saturn, likely a captured Kuiper Belt object. Has a heavily cratered, dark surface.

bodies.name: Uranus
bodies.description: Seventh planet from the Sun. Rotates nearly on its side with an axial tilt of 98 degrees. Coldest planetary atmosphere in the solar system.

bodies.name: Miranda
bodies.description: Smallest and innermost of Uranus's five major moons. Has one of the most extreme and varied topographies in the solar system, including Verona Rupes, the tallest cliff.

bodies.name: Ariel
bodies.description: Fourth-largest moon of Uranus. Brightest of the Uranian moons with the youngest and most geologically active surface.

bodies.name: Umbriel
bodies.description: Third-largest moon of Uranus. Darkest of the major Uranian moons, with a heavily cratered ancient surface.

bodies.name: Titania
bodies.description: Largest moon of Uranus and eighth largest in the solar system. Surface features include canyons, scarps, and impact craters.

bodies.name: Oberon
bodies.description: Second-largest moon of Uranus. Outermost of the five major moons. Has a heavily cratered, old surface with some large fault structures.

bodies.name: Neptune

discovery.discoverer: Johann Galle, Urbain Le Verrier, John Couch Adams

bodies.description: Eighth and farthest planet from the Sun. Has the strongest winds in the solar system, reaching 2,100 km/h.

bodies.name: Triton
bodies.description: Largest moon of Neptune. Only large moon with a retrograde orbit, suggesting it was captured from the Kuiper Belt. Has nitrogen geysers.

bodies.name: Nereid
bodies.description: Third-largest moon of Neptune. Has one of the most eccentric orbits of any known moon in the solar system.

bodies.name: Proteus
bodies.description: Second-largest moon of Neptune. Nearly as large as Mimas but irregularly shaped, the largest irregularly shaped body in the solar system.

bodies.name: Pluto
bodies.description: Largest known dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt. Has a heart-shaped nitrogen ice plain (Sputnik Planitia). Reclassified from planet to dwarf planet in 2006.

bodies.name: Charon
bodies.description: Largest moon of Pluto. So large relative to Pluto that they are sometimes considered a binary system, orbiting a barycenter outside Pluto.

bodies.name: Nix
bodies.description: Third moon of Pluto. Irregularly shaped and has a chaotic rotation.

bodies.name: Hydra
bodies.description: Outermost known moon of Pluto. Irregularly shaped with a highly reflective surface.

bodies.name: Kerberos
bodies.description: Fourth moon of Pluto. Has a double-lobed shape suggesting it may be two objects that merged.

bodies.name: Styx
bodies.description: Smallest known moon of Pluto. Discovered during a search for potential hazards for the New Horizons mission.

bodies.name: Eris

discovery.discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz

bodies.description: Most massive known dwarf planet, 27% more massive than Pluto. Its discovery was a key factor in the reclassification of Pluto.

bodies.name: Dysnomia
bodies.description: Only known moon of Eris. Much darker than Eris itself.

bodies.name: Haumea

discovery.discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz

bodies.description: Elongated dwarf planet in the Kuiper Belt. Fastest known rotation of any large body in the solar system (under 4 hours). Has two moons and a ring system.

bodies.name: Hi'iaka

discovery.discoverer: Mike Brown, A.H. Bouchez, et al.

bodies.description: Larger outer moon of Haumea. Surface covered in water ice.

bodies.name: Namaka

discovery.discoverer: Mike Brown, A.H. Bouchez, et al.

bodies.description: Smaller inner moon of Haumea. About one-tenth the mass of Hi'iaka.

bodies.name: Makemake

discovery.discoverer: Mike Brown, Chad Trujillo, David Rabinowitz

bodies.description: Second-largest known Kuiper Belt object after Pluto. Extremely cold surface covered in methane and ethane ices.

bodies.name: S/2015 (136472) 1 (MK2)
bodies.description: Only known moon of Makemake. Very dark compared to Makemake itself.

bodies.name: Ceres
bodies.description: Largest object in the asteroid belt and the only dwarf planet in the inner solar system. Has bright salt deposits in Occator crater suggesting recent geological activity.

bodies.name: Vesta
bodies.description: Second-largest object in the asteroid belt. Has a differentiated interior with an iron core. Visited by NASA's Dawn spacecraft.

bodies.name: Pallas
bodies.description: Third-largest object in the asteroid belt. Has a highly inclined and eccentric orbit, making it difficult to visit with spacecraft.

bodies.name: Hygiea
bodies.description: Fourth-largest object in the asteroid belt. Nearly spherical, potentially qualifying as a dwarf planet. Largest member of the Hygiea asteroid family.

bodies.name: Juno
bodies.description: One of the larger asteroids in the main belt. An S-type (stony) asteroid with a silicate composition.

bodies.name: Eros
bodies.description: Second-largest near-Earth asteroid. First asteroid to be orbited and landed upon by a spacecraft (NEAR Shoemaker, 2001).

bodies.name: Bennu
bodies.description: Carbonaceous near-Earth asteroid. Target of NASA's OSIRIS-REx mission, which successfully collected and returned a sample to Earth in 2023.

bodies.name: Ryugu
bodies.description: Diamond-shaped carbonaceous near-Earth asteroid. Target of JAXA's Hayabusa2 mission, which returned samples to Earth in 2020.

bodies.name: Itokawa
bodies.description: Small peanut-shaped near-Earth asteroid. First asteroid from which samples were returned to Earth (Hayabusa, 2010).

bodies.name: Psyche
bodies.description: Metal-rich asteroid thought to be the exposed core of a protoplanet. Target of NASA's Psyche mission (launched 2023).

bodies.name: Ida
bodies.description: Main belt asteroid and member of the Koronis family. First asteroid confirmed to have a natural satellite (Dactyl).

bodies.name: Dactyl
bodies.description: Tiny moon of asteroid Ida. First asteroid moon ever discovered, found in images from the Galileo spacecraft flyby.

regions.name: Asteroid Belt
regions.description: Region between Mars and Jupiter containing rocky debris. Contains millions of objects but with a total mass less than 4% of the Moon.

regions.name: Kuiper Belt
regions.description: A circumstellar disc beyond Neptune containing frozen volatiles (ices). Home to Pluto, Haumea, Makemake, and countless smaller icy bodies.

regions.name: Scattered Disc
regions.description: A distant circumstellar disc overlapping the Kuiper Belt but extending much farther with highly eccentric and inclined orbits. Home to Eris.

regions.name: Inner Oort Cloud (Hills Cloud)
regions.description: Theoretical disc-shaped inner region of the Oort cloud. May contain significantly more objects than the outer Oort cloud.

regions.name: Outer Oort Cloud
regions.description: Theoretical spherical shell of icy planetesimals surrounding the solar system. Believed to be the source of long-period comets. Extends to roughly 2 light-years.

regions.name: Heliosphere
regions.description: The vast bubble-like region of space dominated by the Sun's solar wind. Protects the solar system from galactic cosmic rays.

regions.name: Heliopause
regions.description: The boundary where the solar wind is stopped by the interstellar medium. Voyager 1 crossed this boundary at about 121 AU in August 2012.
